title: The Buddha as Exemplar and Archetype
subtitle: Twelve Contemplations on the Path to Awakening
epigraph: Everything arises and passes away. There's nothing in this world worth holding on to.
paragraphs:
Namo tassa bhagavato arahato sammā-sambuddhassa
Namo tassa bhagavato arahato sammā-sambuddhassa
Namo tassa bhagavato arahato sammā-sambuddhassa
Homage to the blessed, noble, and fully self-enlightened one.

What I want to do here is look at the Buddha's life from the point of view of an exemplar. I've chopped it up into twelve different stages so that you can see the passage he went through, his own peculiar journey.

It's said that way back in the distant mists of time, when he was born in the era of a Buddha called Dīpaṅkara, he met this Buddha and put down a cloak to stop him stepping into a puddle. He was so amazed by Dīpaṅkara that he made, there and then, a determination to become a fully self-enlightened Buddha himself.

Now, I don't think it's of any use to discuss whether Dīpaṅkara ever actually existed, because remember: Eastern mythology is very circular. The way you substantiate a leader is by saying it's all happened before. All these Eastern traditions have leaders who came before. The Nigaṇṭhas, for instance — their founder is number so-and-so in a whole line of Nigaṇṭhas which produced the Jain tradition.

But for us, what's important is this act of resolution — an act of resolution to make the process of purification, the process of enlightenment, the full aim of our lives. Total determination, you see. Total determination.

In his case, it was self-enlightened. But although we have this teaching, that doesn't mean we don't become self-enlightened too. Because ultimately it's we who have to do the work. All we can say is that the distinction between us and the Buddha is that he gave us some clues as to which way to go. But the whole process is completely internally dependent on us.

Just to make this resolution once in a lifetime isn't enough, because it has to be constantly reinforced. If you think of any other situation in people's lives — a partnership or a marriage, for instance — they might go through a small ceremony and think that's it. And of course, seven, fifteen years down the line, they're walking away from each other. The problem has been that they haven't recommitted themselves, recommitted themselves and gone through the pain of a relationship. Other people are hell, remember.

The second stage, of course, is that after he made this great resolution, he was then born — it is said — for countless lifetimes, trying to get it right, trying to get the perfections right, trying to purify his heart.

Now again, from our point of view, it's not necessary to believe that he lived thousands of lifetimes. But the myth is telling us that this path is very long, very gradual, and very difficult. I don't want to depress you, but that's what it's telling us: it's okay to make this decision, but actually we've entered into a path which is going to demand everything from us. Everything, eventually.

In the spiritual life, remember, you don't get anything back until you've given up something. And you never know what you're going to get. It's not like you've got an old car that's broken down and you think, well, I'll get rid of it and go and get a new one — and you know which one you're going to get when you drop yours. In the spiritual life, you don't really know at all what you're going to get until you make that leap of faith and give something up. That's the next thing to really consider.
The Buddha, of course, reinforces this in his own teachings. He's constantly telling people: this is gradual. It's gradual. There's no sudden enlightenment.

Now, I know certain schools of Buddhism talk about sudden enlightenment, but it's not sudden in that sense at all. If you talk to Zen monks about their sudden enlightenment, you find they've been meditating for twenty-five years before it came. It's not nothing. You don't get anything for nothing. You have to go through the training.
And this training is twofold: the purification of the heart and the growth of the heart. Heart here meaning our whole disposition.

As you know, as soon as you sit you can see these hindrances, these impurities arising. They're there within deep conditioning in us. Even if you only believe in appearing in this world just this once with birth, we've picked up all this stuff, all this conditioning, and as soon as you come to sit, the stuff begins to arise. The whole process of meditation is the purification of that part of us.

But the other side, remember, is the growth of all these perfections. One of them is resolution — the determination to do what you say you're going to do. That's one of the things you can impress upon yourself just these three days. Once you've set yourself the rhythm of the retreat that you want, you maintain it. That's your discipline, that's your courage, and that's your resolution.

If you fail, of course, you haven't got to get into beating yourself up and saying you're useless. Sometimes you have to have an act of humility to recognise that you've reached too far. It's always good to make sure you're not pushing yourself too much — but on the other hand, you are pushing yourself a bit. As one of my early teachers, Roshi Kennett, used to say: you have to push yourself gently.

Finally, the Buddha's ready for the big thing. He's ready for the full self-enlightenment show. And he's waiting in the Tusita heaven, just waiting for the opportunity to arise. And there it comes: North India, born to a prince, a local governor.

In actuality, he was what you might call a local lord under the king of Kosala. But as it comes to the scriptures, he's this big king in North India. I suppose we would call him upper middle class. And he decides — there's a decision there — to be born.

And here's your classic Freudian trauma: his mother dies. She dies probably because of his birth. And that's somewhere in his heart, isn't it? He loses his mother. About a week after he's born, he's passed on to his mother's sister. And he must have felt that.

So already, you might say, this little child has not only gone through the pangs of rebirth, but the pangs of losing his mother. Already, as it were, he's come into life with real suffering in that heart. The touch of suffering is already there. I might be a bit too Freudian here, but you understand what I'm saying.

And he's obviously precocious. That shouldn't amaze us. Think of somebody like Mozart — I think he wrote his first symphony when he was three or something. When you realise there are little beings who arise who are absolutely unbelievable, it was obvious that this little boy was something special.

The tale always goes — and you get it in other myths — that of course they meet the wise man. And the wise man recognises that here is an extraordinary human being. What he says about this little human being is that either he'll become a world conqueror, a total world conqueror, or a fully self-enlightened person.

Now, that's the same for us. It's not that we're all going to become world conquerors, but the choice is there. Is our life going to be devoted to being great in the world, or to being great spiritually?

And that isn't just a decision we make once at the beginning of our lives. It's constant. Every time we say to ourselves, 'I'm going to have this cup of tea because I want to really enjoy it,' we've chosen the world. If we have a cup of tea in order to investigate the process, with the intention to feed the body rather than feed our emotional life, then we've chosen the spiritual path.

So that division is with us all the time. That's not a problem. In this particular story, it's put at a very heightened level: here we have a little being who could have conquered the whole world or could have become self-enlightened. That's the next thing to contemplate — this idea that all the time the choice is there before us, the choice between the world and spiritual life, between worldly success and our enlightenment.

When his father heard this prophecy, of course, he was in a state of horror. This was his only son — and as far as I know, he remained his only son. Obviously, the father wanted his heir to take the worldly path.

So then we have this part of the myth that tells us his father went to great lengths to make sure his son never suffered. He made sure the boy had all the food he wanted and, as he grew older, all the sex, drugs and rock and roll. As he approaches and gets through his teenage years, he comes to the point of going through all that loveliness of getting married and so on. But there must be something, shall we say, disturbing him even then.

Before we go into that, do remember that the whole of our society is manufactured — our entire economic and political life is designed to draw us into the world. The whole of capitalism, everything we have, is there to make us emboldened in the world, to make us see our life in worldly terms. So he's born into the world and the world wants him. But again, there's this little pain.

And it comes out in the story of his meeting the Devadūta, the messengers from the gods. That's how it's put. So he's out there hunting and doing his thing, killing beasts and all that. And he comes across, in sequence: somebody very sick lying on the road — if you've been to India you know exactly what that means. Somebody very old and crooked and really old. A corpse. And finally, an ascetic sitting under a tree.

Each time he asks, 'What is this?' Because he's been shielded from all this — that's what the myth tells us. He hasn't actually looked at this stuff before. And after they've described it to him, there's the kernel question: Does this happen to me?

So here's a young man, full of energy, full of all that virility, and he suddenly sees sickness, old age, and death. And of course there's this ascetic sitting under a tree, which in the story symbolises hope — the escape from sickness, old age, and death.
For him this obviously becomes a psychological crisis. What we would call today an identity crisis — a proper existential identity crisis. And when does it hit him? Somewhere in his middle twenties.

Now if you think of your own lives and look back to your middle twenties, you'll see there's a change of mentality. There's a sudden realisation that youth is over. That's when people get serious. They get married, they take their job seriously, some even take out pensions. It all happens in that period as you move towards the first ugly inklings of middle age.

So there he is at that point. And he decides he's got to go and really work this out. He's in a psychological crisis and needs to resolve it. The only way he can think of doing so is to join the ascetics, because they are the only people at that time offering anything beyond the worldly life.

He probably spent hours talking to the Brahmins about their philosophy and understandings, because later on he knows it all deeply. He knows all the arguments, and he must have picked it up during that youthful time — he studied the Vedas. So he's already abandoned that scholastic path. Now he wants to abandon the good life as well.

There's another thing that pushes him towards abandoning this life. They've obviously had a big party, and in the morning when he wakes up, there are bodies all around him. There's puke and saliva — it's all horrible. He sees the other side of pleasure, which is, of course, the hangover.

He sees that pleasure is not going to bring that sort of happiness, not going to bring an end to the suffering he feels — this existential suffering of sickness, old age, and ultimately death. It hits hard, doesn't it? Those of you who've had a scrape with death know what he might have been going through. Those of you who've had people die around you know that it can hit you hard.

In fact, the Buddha says there are four types of people. Some wake up — really see that their life has to have a spiritual dimension — upon merely hearing the word 'death'. Some don't wake up until they learn that somebody has died, perhaps on the news. Some don't wake up until somebody very close to them dies. And some unfortunates don't wake up until it's their turn to die. So you don't want to leave it that late.
I always remember Woody Allen saying that the experience of sex is an empty experience — but as empty experiences go...
It's that sort of realisation. And for us, we wouldn't be here now, actually. We wouldn't be in this room at all, any of us, if we hadn't had some inkling around those areas.

But again, it's not something that becomes a huge crisis for everybody. It can be very gradual — a gradual pulling away because of the little bits of suffering that come from pleasure, little bits of realisation.

And of course, our own bodies are our own teachers. That's why I said this morning at the meal time: we're so dependent on the body for our teaching, because it's the body which gets ill, it's the body which grows old, and it's the body which dies.

When we realise that we are embodied — that we are actually in this body, that this body is the way we know the world, the way we experience the world — we suddenly realise, or begin to realise, that we must take care of it. Not smash it around too much.

So these are contemplations. Daily, hourly, moment-to-moment contemplations as things arise. One contemplates, one reflects upon these things. And what it's urging us to do is to abandon, to let go, not to attach to these things.
Remember, when we talk about abandonment, letting go, it's not destruction. We're not talking about not eating. We're talking about eating in a particular way.

Take sleep, for instance. We're not talking about abandoning sleep, because it's a lovely time and we all want to spend some time in oblivion every day. It's recognising that sleep has a purpose: it's there to rest the body. But if we seek happiness in oblivion, then of course the bed constantly beckons us. Every time we feel a bit tired, we launch ourselves onto it. So as you go to bed, you look upon it as a stinking pit. You don't see it as a place where you're going to get pleasure. It's just there to rest the body, just as food is there to nourish the body. These are contemplations to be taken up.
So he's now on the point of leaving. This is what's known as the Great Renunciation.

It's not as though people haven't done this before. Both men and women have had to leave everything to go to war, for instance. People are often drawn out of their style of life for one reason or another. Some are shaken by events and leave everything. Some are kicked out anyway—made redundant and such.

So there comes this point where he abandons, he renounces the worldly life. The story tells us he even saw the birth of his son, so there's that huge attachment to this little child. And yet even so, he gets on his favourite horse, his special horse Kanthaka, with his servant Channa, and off he goes.

These days that can be harshly judged, I think—his abandoning his family and all that. But you must remember, in those days they had very extended families, and I can't imagine he did it without long consultation and argument and all that sort of cajoling.

And then finally, I'm sure he said to them, as I said to my mother, 'I'm only doing it for three years.' When I said that, she disappeared into this cloud of absolute... I said to her, 'I'm going to join the order,' and she just collapsed into this awful weeping. I put my arm around her and said, 'Mum, I'm only going for three years.' And she stopped. It was just because she saw it as an abandonment.

There comes this point where he reaches a river. Here it is: crossing the Rubicon. There's a point where we make that crossing, where we've built up the faith, we've built up the understanding, and finally we make that leap.

Now, in terms of the Buddha, it was a big dramatic moment—it's part of the myth. But for us, it's every day. Every day you have to make that leap of faith. Every day you've got to cross that Rubicon. And it's crossing from what? From the worldly life to the spiritual life.

He cuts off all his hair, which was the custom of those days, throws all his rich clothes away, dons rag robes. He even leaves his horse Kanthaka, who dies of grief. See? It was terrible. But Channa can't leave him—he follows him in the end.

So that's another point of contemplation for us, in our own lives: the idea that we have to really make that commitment some way. That's the whole point of taking refuges and precepts in the Buddhist tradition, of actually joining a specific tradition within Buddhism. It's there in all religions. And that's why it's always ceremonious, why it's always done in community. It's just like a marriage ceremony.

I know people who've been in partnership for a long time. I did a little marriage ceremony for a very old friend of mine—partnership for fifteen years. But then a marriage ceremony was something else. It deepens the commitment to the person. It's a funny thing, but anybody who's been in a long relationship who goes through a marriage ceremony—and I'm not talking about a religious thing, it's a communal declaration, much the way the Quakers do it—it has an effect. It deepens that commitment to the other person as person, as an individual.

So you'll find that in all traditions there is that ceremony of commitment by the person. When I myself became a Buddhist, I did it up at Throssel Hole, and it's five days. Not just a matter of saying a few words—it was a five-day affair, five different ceremonies. And it was all about this business of renouncing, of burning one's karma.

I can't remember the full gist of it now, but by the end of it, you definitely felt you'd joined something. There was that feeling of settlement, that feeling of coming home. That's one of the big feelings in the spiritual life—that finally you've found your tradition, you've found your teacher. And it might, as you know, shift: I didn't stay in the same tradition, I started with Zen. It's not as though it's blocked in time, that you can't move. But there's that feeling of grounding yourself finally in a spiritual practice.

So then he joins the great meditation traditions of the time and goes through two teachers. What these teachers teach him is how to establish ecstasy within himself. And that means you don't actually need the world.

Through the practices of samatha meditation—concentration meditation, mantra, what we call kasinas (images that you internalise)—one begins to recognise that you can actually attain enormous ecstasy within your own heart without any help from the outside world at all.

You don't need sex, drugs, rock and roll. You don't need cinema, you don't need anything. You can sit there and develop this enormous state within yourself, because that's the capability of the mind and heart. And once you've established it on an object and internalised it, you don't even need that object.

One of the main methods is using the breath—so you just need the body, as it were. All you need is, in India anyway, a bit of shelter, a tree, a bit of clothes, somebody to give you a bit of rice every day, and you sit in ecstasy. That's it. You can imagine why it was attractive. This is one of the attractions of the monastic life for those who can practise it. It's not within everybody's gift.

But every time he came out of these states, he found himself back in dear old Gautama, back in dear old Siddhartha, lumped with all this suffering. It wasn't an answer to sickness, old age, and death. It wasn't an answer to his existential crisis. It was just another way of displacing his life into a cloud of beauty—a pink cloud. Lovely when he was there, but pretty horrible when he came out. And of course you can get addicted to that like any drug.

As soon as he began to realise this, he recognised that this wasn't the path. Each time, it's said, he was so brilliant at his meditation that the teacher would ask him to be a teacher alongside him. And each time he said, 'I can't—I'm not fully satisfied. There must be something else.'

So here's the first thing to understand: when beautiful states arise in your meditation, fine—we're not trying to get rid of them, we're not trying to destroy beauty or happiness. But that's not the end of the path, that's not what it's about. The state arises, fantastic, and then you notice it passing away. When you see yourself grasping for it as it passes, when you come back to the meditation hall intending to re-establish that state, know that you've fallen into a relationship of attachment—which could cause suffering, which could cause dissatisfaction.

Abandoning that approach, he went into self-mortification. The psychology of self-mortification rests on the idea that all suffering is bodily based. If you think about it, most of it is. Where do you experience suffering if not in the body? The hunger, wanting to eat, wanting to see something. Just the physical pain of the body.
Self-mortification, especially the practice he chose—starving oneself—reduces the body to, shall we say, almost a skeletal state where suffering is lessened.

For those of you who've done a ten-day fast, I don't know whether you experienced this, but normally the first three days are horrible—all these horrible things are pouring out of the body. But you keep drinking water, and then suddenly there comes this place where it's absolutely beautiful. And it remains like that. The body doesn't feel hungry. Of course it's easing itself, but it doesn't feel hungry. Your passions are down. You don't feel passionate about anything. Everything is lovely and subdued, beautifully subdued.

You can go on like that until you get close to death, and I think it begins to hurt. So the idea is just to eat enough to maintain that state—the body not exciting the body—because excitement has its base in the senses.
But all that happened was that he obviously reached these points of suffering, and there came a point where we could say he despaired. He despaired.

That will come to us too—the sense of despair about our practice. It comes because of expectation. It comes because we think we should be somewhere else than where we are. There will be these little moments in our spiritual life where we think, 'Nothing's happening, I'm not getting anywhere,' and all this sort of stuff. They're little jabs of despair. So you have to be careful of those.

He himself abandoned that self-mortification business, and with it his five disciples left him—they said he'd gone soft. So he wanders off by himself, and he's sitting on the roadside when a woman comes along with a bowl of rice pudding, which is a very sacred food in Buddhism.

She sees this monk by the roadside, and the myth tells us she thought it was a god and offered this food to a god. But I think we can realistically say that what she saw was somebody on death's door and looking pretty sad. She was probably carrying it to some family as a gift, and when she had a little chat with him she said, look, you'd better have this rice pudding.

So he eats this rice pudding and of course it lifts his body, lifts his spirits. And he can't let go of the idea that there must be an end to suffering. It's there within him; the kernel of the idea is within him. He must have been reviewing his life and everything he'd been through. Perhaps he's wandered past the three years he promised his family and he's thinking he might go back.

As he's reviewing, he remembers a point in childhood. He must have been about six, seven, maybe eight, and he's watching his father during the ploughing ceremony, just watching his father. And for some reason, he remembers the quality of that watching—something he obviously hadn't been aware of, either through the jhānic meditations, the absorption meditations, or through all those practices of self-mortification.

If you think about it, that's quite reasonable, because all those practices are about gaining something, getting something. You're doing something for something else. I'm doing the jhāna in order to get over sickness, old age and death. I'm self-mortifying in order to get over. So it's always drawing that person out of the present moment.
But here he remembers this point where he's watching his father and he's just in the present moment.

Now if you think of children, especially around the age of two, three, four, you see it comes naturally. They see a bug and they just absorb into it. They don't have the language to think about it. They just absorb into it out of total interest. They're completely there.

One of the signs that a child is in that state—and we ourselves when we're in that state—is the jaw drops. You get parents like my old man used to tell me to shut my mouth. 'Shut your mouth, son.' You see kids like that, don't you? What it is, you see, is that when there's no jaw tension, when there's no tongue movement, there's no thought. If you're in your meditation you might catch that. When a thought comes there's a vibration on the tongue because it's just so intimately connected.

So it's just like a child, absorbed into that. And then after the absorption, after that business of taking in the image, there's a stop and the child turns around and says, 'What is it?' So mum says, 'It's a slug.'

From that point on, there's a title, there's a name—it's a slug. That child will never look at a slug again. What they will see is the image they had of a slug imposed upon the slug they now see. And that's the whole distortion that conceptual thinking does for us.

To get back through all that language and all that conception, back to the original mind, back to that original way of seeing things—that is the process of enlightenment. Because once we're back there we're back to that amazing intelligence that we have, which is now informed. See, that's the paradox. It's been informed through language. We couldn't have got here without language. We couldn't have got here without the teaching. But now the intelligence is informed. It clarifies itself of all those conceptual constructs and it sees anew.

So here's the Buddha. He remembers that moment and he's so inspired by it. And remember, he's despaired. He knows now this is it because he doesn't see anything else. He's been through all the traditions. He's done everything. The world can't offer him anything else.
So he approaches the tree we now call the Bodhi tree. Interestingly enough, the leaves are shaped like a heart, are they not? He sits under this big leafy tree by the side of the river.

And he makes this great determination. We've had the great renunciation; now we have the great determination. Right, I've done everything, this is it. I'm going to sit here and crack this problem or I will die. Or I will die. That's the commitment.

It was interesting—one of my teachers came to the Vihara in Birmingham when I'd been ordained for about four or five years. I'd been practising a little bit in India, I remember, but I was living at the Vihara anyway. And I'd been to Burma before and got very ill with amoebic dysentery and amoebic hepatitis and all sorts of things.

He wanted me to join him, you see, and he sent one of the monks to tell me. 'Ajahn Anita wants you to come and join him.' So I said, 'Well, frankly, the last time I was in Burma, I nearly died. I really don't want to go east again.'
So he went back, and he came back with a message: 'If you're not prepared to die, forget it.' Obviously he was mirroring back to me the memory of the Buddha.
So there's the Buddha. He's now made this great determination. For us, it's little bits—making that determination, determination.

Every time you sit, one of the big mistakes we make is that because it's become habitual, we just take a seat. We just sit here. And of course, nothing much happens. The mind wanders, we get caught up in it, and we might feel a bit more relaxed.

Every time you sit, you have to re-establish that commitment. That's your determination. It's not a great determination; we don't have to worry too much about death. It's a great determination here, now—the great determination to commit ourselves one hundred and one percent to this moment of meditation. And that's when you get the fruit. That's when you get the fruits of your practice.
As the story goes, of course, he did become enlightened. It took him six hours. There's your standard. If you can sit six hours, you'll probably make it.
He became enlightened at that point. He then spends the first night—the first three nights—trying to understand what he's seen.

When we chant in the morning, I chant the Wheel of Dependent Origination. That's the psychology, that's what he understood—it tells us about why we suffer, the psychology of suffering. And I do it three times to commemorate that moment, because he went forward on it, then he saw how it came back on itself, and then he did it again, forward and back, to make sure he had fully understood the process. That's what that chanting is about. It commemorates that moment.
And that victory verse is what he was supposed to have told Ānanda—what he spontaneously spoke when he became enlightened.
Now, having made this moment of realisation—understanding what the whole thing was, what is—what comes next? What's the next thought that arises?
Who can I teach?

With wisdom, compassion arises naturally. There was no break. It wasn't, "Ah, I've done it, fine, great, get back into bed." It didn't end there. There was this immediate movement out of the heart: Who can I teach?

And of course, there's that little doubt. Again, it's put mythically: the great Brahmā Sahampati comes down and says to him, "Look, there are some with only a little dust in their eyes. There are people who will understand this stuff."
With that reflection, he looks at the world and thinks, who is going to understand this? When he considers his old teachers, he realises they're dead, they've gone. So there's a sadness.

I saw this lovely statue in Burma the last time I was there—I'd never seen anything like it before. He sat in posture like this, but one hand pointed to his heart like this. I said to them, "What's the meaning of that?" I'd never seen that mudrā, that posture. They said it represents the sadness he felt when he realised his teachers had died.

Then he thinks about those five disciples—remember, the ones who'd called him a scab and a deserter, turned away from him, and gone off. And he wanders off to find them, walking well over a hundred miles. It's not as though he goes next door and says, "Hey, listen, I'm enlightened now, so I'll give you the message." He walks. He goes in search. The teacher goes out in search.

Often it's put in the tradition that the pupil searches for the teacher—that when the teacher's ready, the pupil arises, ready to receive the teaching. And of course, we in our search have to search for our teacher. But the teacher also has to be ready, has to go out and search. Although I must say, the Indian tradition tends to be for somebody who feels they're ready to sit under a tree and just wait. If somebody does come along, that's the test of it—they must be drawn into the teaching.

Now, every one of us who've meditated knows this: as soon as you tell somebody you've meditated, they expect you to be somehow different. They say, "Well, what's the effect? What's it done for you?" And as soon as you open your mouth, you're teaching them.
If you go to people and say, "Well, I go to Buddhist meditation, yeah, it's fantastic," and you're slowly getting drunk on bitter beer, then people are going to say, "Well, that's very interesting."

As soon as you take up a spiritual path, you take responsibility. And if you don't behave according to a certain standard that the practice demands of you—again, don't get into big false guilt syndromes—but of course you're undermining other people's spirituality because they become cynical. Whether you like it or not, you take on a certain responsibility.

Having taken on that responsibility, he searches for his disciples. And of course, he meets them. As it's said, as soon as they saw him coming, they realised something had changed about him. They weren't going to make a seat for him or anything—but as soon as they saw him coming, they thought, "No, there's something strange."

And that's when he gives the first talk about his teaching. The whole teaching life begins from that moment. He goes on, as you know, to establish an institution, to gather people around him, and eventually to have an enormous effect on human civilisation.

But interestingly enough, that's not the full end of the story. Because in the scriptures, it says that Mara, the evil one, actually throws doubt at him. Mara attacks with all his armies—and this happened just after the Enlightenment.
The question is: who are you to say that you are enlightened? Who are you to say that you've finished the path? How do you know that?
So even when he made this huge realisation, there was still the doubt: is this really the end of it? Is this really the end of suffering?
It's not as though the Buddhist teaching is about believing what he said. We know that. It's not a belief system—it's a practical teaching. We put it into practice and it makes sense.

But the doubt, especially in ourselves—that often comes: everybody else can do it but not me. Be very careful when doubt comes up to make sure you don't get caught up in that thought, that you actually just stay with that position of not knowing. And that's very difficult for us, because the self loves to be in control. It loves to know. We all want to know what we're going to do at the end of this course. We've all got it worked out what's going to happen when we leave this place. If you didn't, there'd be this anxiety: what am I going to do?

So he touched the earth as a witness to his enlightenment. You'll often find that statue with his hand touching the earth. And basically, I suppose it's a call to people to say: well, you try and prove me wrong. Because in my understanding, in my experience, there is now no more suffering in me. I don't experience suffering at any level.
That's not physical suffering, for those of you wondering. The body always has pain. It's the heart. There's no more of all this depression, anxiety, all the gamut of human misery. That's all gone.

But interestingly enough, for seven years Mara hunts him. It's a curious thing in the scriptures that we have this figure of Mara. Now, you can either see it as some sort of devil, but you can also see it as traces within the Buddha's mind—little traces still there. So there's been this huge enlightenment, but little traces remain.

And Mara sends his three daughters: Lust, Sensual Pleasure, and Boredom. So he obviously had a big problem with lust. If you compare that with Jesus Christ, it's interesting, because Christ seems to have been tempted by power, doesn't he? Took him up to the mountain.
So those are your worldly pursuits: fame, friendship, riches, power, and of course seeking pleasure in the sensual world.
The Buddha had had a good life, hadn't he? All that pleasure. And Mara's three daughters kept tempting him.

Now interestingly enough, why was boredom there? Because boredom is part of that pleasure syndrome. The more pleasure you have, the more it becomes obsolescent—you've always got to go for a bigger kick. What moves you is the boredom of where you are. So boredom is the great motivator towards addiction.
Whenever you're bored—bored in your meditation—you never feed the boredom by distraction. You undermine boredom by repetition.

So when you're sitting there watching the breath and there's just this huge thing—this is so boring—you feel the boredom, you know it's there, but you just keep watching the breath. Eventually it fades away, because you're not feeding it. You know it's there. Remember, you've got to feel these things. You've got to actually experience them. You mustn't push them away.

There comes a point, of course—and it's said seven years, which is one of these mythical cycles, isn't it? Seven years. His daughters haven't been successful. Mara sees that he just can't get through to the Buddha, can't tempt him into all these things again. So he says to his daughters, look, let's forget it. It's not working. And his daughters say, well, let's have just one more go, Dad.
But eventually he slinks off. And what does the daughter say? Why are you going? Why are you abandoning this quest?
And Mara says, the Buddha sees me.

It is seeing—seeing what all the world has to offer as vanity, as emptiness—that undermines its power over us. By that time, it would seem that whatever was there by ancient conditioning was completely drained out over those seven years.
And then finally, what can we expect from this sort of training?

There's this lovely tale in the scriptures where somebody says to the Buddha, look, this training you're going on about is bloody difficult. And he said, it is. It's hard. You've got to commit yourself and all that. And the man said, okay, so what do you get? Nibbana, so what? What do you get when you get to Nibbana?
He says, when you achieve Nibbana, you are happy and with it contented. You are joyful and with it contented. And he's talking about himself.

When it comes to his own time to die, there's a lovely scripture where basically he just waits for the body to die. He's lying there on his side, and the people have come around him. Even at the end, he's ready to take somebody into the order, isn't he? Right at the end, right before he dies, he's ready to accept somebody.
And there he is, dying—and most of us are freaked out, eh? Most of us don't want anybody around. But here's this lovely peace, just this letting go of the body. Letting go of the body.
And of course, there was great sadness from the younger monks. They all started crying—the nuns too. And the elders said, what do you expect? Everything arises and passes away.
That was one of the Buddha's pet sayings: everything arises and passes away. There's nothing in this world worth holding on to. Nothing worth holding on to.
I hope that's been of some use to you, in terms of how to reflect upon your own spiritual life.
